definitionShort: A system that ranks every lead by how likely they are to convert, typically across three dimensions: Fit (ICP match), Intent (engagement), Quality (data reliability).
definitionLong: Lead scoring assigns every lead a grade based on structured signals — firmographic match, engagement behaviour, and data completeness. Modern scoring uses three independent scores (Fit, Intent, Quality), each 0–100, which combine into a Grade A–F. Unlike qualification (a one-time yes/no gate), scoring is continuous — the score updates every time a signal arrives (a call, a WhatsApp reply, a website re-visit) and decays when leads go silent.
examples: A Grade A lead matches your ICP, has recent engagement, and complete data.
A Grade F lead has a Quality Score below 20 (junk phone, fake email).

definitionShort: The highest-priority lead grade. In Leadkaun, a Grade A requires Fit ≥ 65, Intent ≥ 60, and Quality ≥ 60. Rep SLA: call within 24 hours.
definitionLong: Grade A represents the hottest, most qualified leads in your pipeline. A Grade A lead matches your Ideal Customer Profile, has shown recent buying intent, and has reliable data. Grade A leads should be worked first every day — they have 3–5× higher close rates than lower grades. Response time matters: Grade A leads contacted within 6 hours convert at ~4× the rate of 24-hour callbacks.
examples: Real estate enquiry from a Tier-1 city with clear budget + recent WhatsApp engagement.
EdTech parent who filled a form and booked a counsellor call.

definitionShort: The second-highest lead grade — a strong lead that needs nurture. Fit ≥ 55, Intent ≥ 40, Quality ≥ 50. Rep SLA: follow up within 48 hours.
definitionLong: Grade B leads are qualified and interested but not quite hot. They might have a longer sales cycle, partial fit data, or paused engagement. Grade B leads become Grade A when Intent spikes (e.g. a WhatsApp reply) or Fit improves (e.g. budget signal confirmed). Grade B leads should be worked within 48 hours and nurtured on a weekly cadence.
examples: A lead who had a great first call but is now comparing options.
An ICP-fit prospect who hasn't replied yet but visited your pricing page.

definitionShort: A nurture-cadence lead. Fit ≥ 40, Intent ≥ 25, Quality ≥ 35. Weekly touches; not daily priority.
definitionLong: Grade C leads are qualified but early-stage. They might be researching, not ready to buy, or partially aligned with your ICP. Grade C leads should be on a weekly nurture cadence — not ignored, but not consuming rep time that should go to Grade A. A Grade C can jump to Grade B or A when engagement signals improve.
examples: Early-stage B2B SaaS lead who downloaded a whitepaper.
Real estate enquiry from a 2nd-tier city with partial contact info.

definitionShort: Junk leads. Quality Score below 20. Test strings, fake phones, or unusable data. Discard.
definitionLong: Grade F marks leads whose data is unusable. Phone doesn't match Indian carrier formats. Email fails MX check. Company name reads 'asdfghjkl'. Working a Grade F lead is actively worse than skipping it — it burns rep time that should go to real leads. Leadkaun automatically marks Quality < 20 leads as Grade F; reps don't see them in the Priority Queue.
examples: Form submission with phone number '9999999999'.
Enquiry text that says only 'call me'.

definitionShort: A 0–100 score measuring how well a lead matches your Ideal Customer Profile. Weights: industry (30) + geography (20) + business type (20) + role (15) + budget (15).
definitionLong: Fit Score is the static component of lead scoring — it tells you whether a lead is a good match for your product, independent of how engaged they are. Leadkaun's default weights favour industry match (30%) and geography (20%), reflecting the reality that Indian B2B sales often gates on vertical expertise and regional presence. You can customise weights per ICP on the Growth tier.
examples: A real-estate team in Bengaluru matches 'industry=real-estate' (30) + 'geography=tier-1' (20) + 'business-type=agency' (20) = 70 Fit.

definitionShort: A 0–100 score measuring how engaged a lead is, with time-based decay. Source baseline + call (+15) + WhatsApp reply (+10) + meeting (+25) — minus 3 points/day after engagement threshold.
definitionLong: Intent Score captures buying readiness. Unlike a static score, Intent rises when leads engage (replies, meetings, website revisits) and decays when they go silent. The decay is critical — it's what makes 'stale Grade A' automatically drop to Grade B without manager intervention. Intent Score floor is the source baseline (a referral starts higher than a cold ad click).
examples: A lead who replies to WhatsApp gains +10. If silent for 5 days, drops −15.
A meeting-booked signal is +25 — often enough to push Grade C to Grade A.

definitionShort: A 0–100 score measuring how reliable a lead's data is. Valid phone (30) + email (15) + company (15) + inquiry clarity (20) + source reliability (10) — minus junk penalties.
definitionLong: Quality Score is the gate against junk. A lead with a valid Indian phone number (E.164 format, correct carrier range) and a real company name scores high. A lead with test strings or obvious bad data scores low. Leads under 20 Quality are marked Grade F and excluded from the Priority Queue automatically.
examples: Complete form: phone valid + email valid + company 'Reliance Retail' + inquiry 'looking for 500 seats of CRM' → Quality 95.
Bad form: phone '000' + no email → Quality 5 → Grade F.

definitionShort: The automatic drop in a lead's Intent Score over time with no engagement — typically −3 points per day after an engagement threshold.
definitionLong: Intent Decay is what keeps the Priority Queue honest. Without decay, old hot leads stay at Grade A forever. With decay, a lead that hit Grade A a week ago but hasn't engaged since automatically drops to Grade B or C, without any manager intervention. When the lead re-engages, Intent spikes back up. Decay floors at the source baseline so a referral never drops below a cold ad click.
examples: Grade A lead goes silent 5 days → Intent −15 → drops to Grade B.
Re-engagement (WhatsApp reply) spikes Intent +10 → back to Grade A.

definitionShort: A ranked list of leads per rep, re-ranked in real time as signals arrive — telling the rep exactly who to call next.
definitionLong: The Priority Queue is what replaces 'which lead should I call first?' with a decisive answer. It ranks every open lead by a composite score: grade + overdue callbacks + engagement trend + recency. A new Grade A arrival instantly moves to the top. Intent decay pushes stale leads down. Reps don't decide — they work top-down. Managers stop micromanaging because the queue is the plan.
examples: Rep opens the app at 9:30 AM; top of queue is 'Priya, Grade A, site visit due today, ₹5L GCV'.
WhatsApp reply at 11 AM re-ranks that lead to #1 instantly.

definitionShort: A stale lead that passed its grade-appropriate SLA with a rupee value attached — 'Grade A, 48 hours stale, ₹12L at risk'.
definitionLong: Missed Opportunity translates 'overdue follow-up' into a rupee number. A Grade A lead with no contact in 24+ hours carries an expected loss based on industry average deal value × grade conversion rate. Aggregated across open leads, this becomes '₹ at risk this week' — the single number that drives every Monday manager review in Leadkaun-running teams.
examples: 8 Grade A real estate leads stale 48h × ₹4.5L each = ₹36L at risk.
Rep Morning Brief: '₹1.8L to recover today — call Priya, Rajesh, Mohan'.

definitionShort: The total rupee value of stale leads across your pipeline — the leading indicator of revenue leakage.
definitionLong: ₹ at Risk is the aggregate cost of every stale lead in your pipeline. Computed as (avg deal value × grade-specific conversion rate) summed across all overdue Grade A, B, and C leads. For a typical 10-rep Indian B2B SMB, baseline ₹ at Risk sits at ₹2–5 lakh/week once Leadkaun has been running for 30 days. When a team first sees the number, it's often ₹8–15 lakh because backlog has accumulated.
examples: 10-rep EdTech team, 40 Grade A stale at ₹16.5k each = ₹6.6L at risk this week.
Same team after 30 days on Leadkaun: ₹2.1L at risk, ₹4.5L recovered.

definitionShort: A daily 8:30 AM IST email to each rep and manager — ₹ at risk today, top Grade A leads, overdue follow-ups.
definitionLong: The Morning Brief is the ritual that turns lead scoring into a daily habit. Every weekday at 8:30 AM IST, each rep receives an email listing their top 5 Grade A leads, ₹ at risk today, and overdue follow-ups. Managers get a parallel brief with per-rep rollups. The Brief sets the day before stand-up, coffee, or any other tool. By 11 AM, the hot leads from overnight are contacted or on recovery paths.
examples: Rep brief: '4 Grade A enquiries replied overnight. ₹6L in admissions at risk if not called by 11 AM.'
Manager brief: 'Priya ₹1.8L, Rajesh ₹1.2L, Mohan ₹1.2L. Team total: ₹4.2L.'

definitionShort: A lead whose Intent Score has crossed the sales-ready threshold — typically triggering an immediate alert to the assigned rep.
definitionLong: A Sales-Qualified Lead (SQL) is a lead that has demonstrated enough buying intent to warrant immediate sales engagement. In Leadkaun, SQL is the moment Intent Score crosses a configurable threshold — typically when a lead hits Grade A combined with a recent meaningful signal (meeting booked, concrete question). When the SQL threshold is crossed, the rep gets an alert within 60 seconds. This closes the response-time gap that kills most hot leads.
examples: Grade B lead books a demo call → Intent +25 → crosses SQL threshold → rep alert within 60s.
WhatsApp reply 'I want to buy' triggers intent-specific rule → SQL.

definitionShort: A lead qualified by marketing behaviour (downloads, webinar signups) but not yet sales-ready.
definitionLong: An MQL has engaged with marketing (content downloads, webinar attendance, pricing page visits) enough to warrant nurture, but hasn't shown direct buying intent. In the B2B funnel, MQL is upstream of SQL. For Indian B2B SMBs with thin marketing motions, MQL may be a smaller category — most leads arrive through paid ads, referrals, or aggregators with clearer intent signals.
examples: Downloaded an ROI calculator — MQL.
Booked a demo call — SQL (beyond MQL).

definitionShort: The firmographic profile of your best-fit customers. Drives the Fit Score component of lead scoring.
definitionLong: ICP defines who you sell to best. It typically captures: industry, company size, geography, role of primary buyer, and budget tier. Leadkaun's default ICP templates are pre-configured for 12 Indian B2B verticals (real estate, EdTech, BFSI, etc.) so teams can onboard without defining ICP from scratch. You can customise weights per vertical on the Growth tier.
examples: EdTech ICP: Indian K-12 school, 500+ students, admissions head as buyer, ₹5L+ annual software spend.
Real estate ICP: Tier-1 city, 5-30 rep agency, developer builder as partner.

definitionShort: A new category of sales software that shapes rep behaviour in real time — not just records activity. Leadkaun is India's first Sales Behaviour OS.
definitionLong: A Sales Behaviour Operating System sits between lead arrival and deal closure and actively reshapes what reps do next. Unlike a CRM (which records what happened) or a sales engagement tool (which automates outbound), a Sales Behaviour OS combines grading + priority queueing + ₹-at-risk surfacing + daily rituals (Morning Brief) to change the rep's day-to-day decisions. The focus is behaviour change, not data capture.
examples: CRM: 'rep logged 30 calls this week'.
Sales Behaviour OS: 'rep recovered ₹3.2L from Grade A leads this week'.

definitionShort: Software that records interactions between a sales team and its leads/customers. Focus is on data capture, not behaviour change.
definitionLong: A CRM centralises the record of every interaction with every lead — calls, emails, meetings, notes, deal stages. Popular CRMs in India include Zoho CRM, LeadSquared, HubSpot, Salesforce, Freshsales. CRMs are excellent at recording; they are not designed to reshape what reps do next. That job is what a Sales Behaviour OS fills. Most teams run CRM + Sales Behaviour OS as complementary layers.
examples: Zoho CRM: comprehensive customisation, weak on rep adoption.
Salesforce: enterprise-grade, overkill for Indian SMBs.

definitionShort: A buying-readiness signal captured from a WhatsApp reply — 70% of Indian B2B leads first-contact on WhatsApp, making it the primary intent channel.
definitionLong: WhatsApp is the dominant first-contact channel for Indian B2B sales. Treating WhatsApp replies as first-class intent signals — not as an integration afterthought — is essential. In Leadkaun, reps log WhatsApp interactions in 3 taps (stage + intent + outcome), which feeds the Intent Score in real time. A high-intent WhatsApp reply can push a Grade C lead to Grade A in under 500ms.
examples: Parent WhatsApps 'can you send brochure?' at 9 PM → logged as 'asking questions + high intent' → +10 Intent.
Silent for 5 days → Intent decays −15.

definitionShort: The design rule that every lead interaction — call, WhatsApp, meeting — must be logged in 3 taps or less, without typing.
definitionLong: 3-Tap Logging is the adoption hack that keeps Indian field reps using the tool. Most CRMs require 4–6 clicks and a modal to log a call. Leadkaun's 3-tap logging is: tap stage (first-reply, asking-questions, objection, meeting-booked, ghosted), tap intent (high/medium/low/negative), tap outcome (progressed/stuck/closed). Total time: 10 seconds. Reps don't break flow. Data capture rate goes from 30% to 80%.
examples: Rep finishes a call, taps 'Progressed + High intent + Meeting booked' — 8 seconds.
WhatsApp reply logged with 3 taps mid-conversation.

definitionShort: The product-design principle that Leadkaun must be useful from day one with zero rep logging — grading and queueing work on ingested data alone.
definitionLong: Zero-Input Mode is the India-first design constraint that says: our product must deliver value even if reps log nothing. The scoring engine, Priority Queue, and Missed Opportunity Engine all run on ingested lead data (from CSV, Google Sheets, forms) without requiring rep input. Rep logging then layers on top to make it better — but it's not a prerequisite for value. This is how Leadkaun achieves 80%+ adoption rates where CRMs get 30%.
examples: Day 1 after onboarding: 600 leads imported from Google Sheet, all graded A–F, Priority Queue populated — rep has their day planned without logging anything.
Rep starts logging calls in week 2 — scores refine further.

definitionShort: Indian numbering unit equal to 100,000. Used throughout Indian B2B sales reporting — e.g. ₹5 lakh, ₹18 lakh.
definitionLong: 1 lakh = 100,000. Indian sales teams report revenue, deal values, and pipeline in lakhs rather than millions. Leadkaun's UI uses lakhs/crores throughout — reports, alerts, Morning Briefs — rather than converting everything to millions. This is a design choice: localising UI to Indian numbering avoids the mental translation tax that US-first CRMs impose on Indian users.
examples: A deal worth ₹5,00,000 reads as ₹5 lakh.
Monthly pipeline of ₹1,80,00,000 reads as ₹1.8 crore.

definitionShort: Indian numbering unit equal to 10 million (100 lakh). Used for large deal values and annual revenue — e.g. ₹1.5 crore.
definitionLong: 1 crore = 10,000,000 = 100 lakh. Crore is the scale for annual revenue, property transactions, large corporate deals, and enterprise contracts. Leadkaun's reports and dashboards use crore consistently for values above ₹1 crore, rather than switching to 'million'. This matches how Indian founders and sales leaders think about business numbers.
examples: Series A funding of ₹15 crore = ₹15,00,00,000.
Annual revenue of ₹50 crore.

definitionShort: Software features that automate repetitive sales tasks — lead grading, follow-up scheduling, alerting — without removing human touch.
definitionLong: Sales automation in the Leadkaun context means: automatic lead grading on arrival, automatic Priority Queue re-ranking as signals arrive, automatic follow-up cadence generation per grade, automatic alerts on SQL crossings. It does NOT mean automated outbound calling or automated WhatsApp sending — those remain human actions. Automation handles the decisions; humans handle the conversations.
examples: New lead arrives → graded A–F in 500ms automatically.
Grade A stale 24h → alert fires to rep automatically.

definitionShort: A grade-aware follow-up schedule — Grade A gets 3 touches in week 1; Grade B gets weekly; Grade C gets 2-week nurture.
definitionLong: Nurture Cadence is the schedule of touches applied to a lead based on grade. Leadkaun auto-generates the cadence when a lead is graded, and rep sees the next touch in the Priority Queue with the reason ('Follow-up 2 of 3 — Grade A, first call 3 days ago'). Completing a touch logs the outcome and schedules the next. Missing a touch drops the Follow-up Score and triggers manager visibility.
examples: Grade A real estate: T+0 call, T+6h WA, T+24h call.
Grade C B2B SaaS: 2-week nurture with product content.

definitionShort: A 0–100 per-rep metric: on-time follow-up completions divided by assigned follow-ups × 100.
definitionLong: The Follow-up Score tells a manager how disciplined a rep is with their cadence. A rep who completes all assigned follow-ups on time holds at 100. Missing a follow-up drops the score proportionally. Rescheduling with a reason code doesn't penalise; silent misses do. Managers see the Follow-up Score on the Rep Performance card, alongside ₹ recovered and Grade A contact time.
examples: Rep assigned 20 follow-ups this week, completes 18 on time → Follow-up Score 90.
Consistently 100 → rep is trusted to run autonomously.

definitionShort: An Analytics Dashboard card ranking every lead source by ₹ won — not by lead count — so agencies that send junk get dethroned by ones that send Grade A.
definitionLong: Source Truth is the dashboard card that answers 'which agency/channel is actually worth paying for?'. Instead of ranking sources by lead volume (which rewards junk senders), Source Truth ranks by ₹ won from that source over the last 90 days. The top-ranked source might send 50 leads a month (not 500), but they're Grade A + close at 15%. Managers use this monthly to kill bad spend fast.
examples: '99acres: ₹3.2Cr closed last quarter'.
'Agency X: 500 leads sent, ₹0 closed — kill the spend'.

definitionShort: An Analytics card showing stale leads by ₹ value and by rep — manager's 1:1 prep material.
definitionLong: Loss Analysis breaks down the ₹-at-risk pool by rep, by source, and by industry. A manager uses it Monday morning to prepare weekly 1:1s with data in hand: 'Priya, your ₹ at risk is ₹1.8L this week — let's walk through the top 5'. No guesswork, no accusations — just numbers. Over time, Loss Analysis patterns reveal whether staleness is a rep discipline issue, a source quality issue, or a cadence design issue.
examples: Priya: ₹1.8L at risk, 80% from Grade A overdue.
Source X: 30% stale rate → audit for quality.

definitionShort: A catch-all queue for leads that aren't yet assigned to a rep — manager-visible, bulk-assignable.
definitionLong: Leads flow into Leadkaun faster than assignment rules can always perfectly route them. The Unassigned Queue captures the remainder: ambiguous ICP matches, geography outside default territories, manual entries without assignment. The manager sees the Unassigned Queue on their dashboard and can bulk-assign in a single click. Unassigned leads aren't invisible — they're just waiting.
examples: Out-of-geography lead arrives → Unassigned Queue → manager routes to nearest rep.
Bulk CSV upload of 500 leads → 50 land Unassigned → 1-click route.
